Generally, there are two approaches to planning, i.e., sectoral planning and regional planning. Sectoral planning means formulation and implementation of the sets of schemes or programmes aimed at development of various sectors of the economy, such as agriculture,... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: PLANNING AND, SUSTAINABLE, DEVELOPMENT IN, INDIAN CONTEXT. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Water Resources is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that which reforms and reconstruction are often undertaken.

The text explains that generally, there are two approaches to planning, i.e., sectoral planning and regional planning.

The text explains that sectoral planning means formulation and implementation of the sets of schemes or programmes aimed at development of various sectors of the economy, such as agriculture, irrigation, manufacturing, power, construction, transport, communication, social infrastructure and services.

The text explains that there is no uniform economic development over space in any country.

The text explains that some areas are more developed and some lag behind.

The text explains that this uneven pattern of development over space necessitates that the planners have a spatial perspective and draw the plans to reduce regional imbalance in development.

The text explains that this type of planning is termed as regional planning.

The text explains that unit III Chapter 6 PLANNING AND SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT IN INDIAN CONTEXT The word ‘planning’ is not new to you as it is a part of everyday usage.

The text explains that you must have used it with reference to preparation for your examination or visit to a hill station.

The text explains that it involves the process of thinking, formulation of a scheme or programme and implementation of a set of actions to achieve some goal.

The text explains that though it is a very broad term, in this chapter, it has been used with reference to the process of economic development.

The text explains that it is, thus different from the traditional hit-and-miss methods by On 1 January 2015, the NITI Aayog was formed.

The text explains that india adopted centralised planning after Independence, but subsequently, it graduated into decentralised multi-level planning.

The text explains that the responsibility of plan formulation was with the Planning Comminssion at the Centre, State and district levels.

The text explains that but on 1 January 2015, the Planning Commision was replaced by the NITI Aayog.

The text explains that nITI Aayog has been set up with the objective of involving the states in economic policy making for India for providing strategic and technical advice to the Central and State governments.

The text explains that but later on, it emphasised on irrigation projects, land development programmes, afforestation, grassland development and creation of basic rural infrastructure, such as electricity, roads, market, credit and services.

The text explains that since growing population pressure is forcing the society to utilise the marginal lands for agriculture, and, thereby causing ecological degradation, there is a need to create alternative employment opportunities in the drought- prone areas.

The text explains that broadly, the drought- prone area in India spread over semi-arid and arid tract of Rajasthan, Gujarat, Western Madhya Pradesh, Marathwada region of Maharashtra, Rayalseema and Telangana plateaus of Andhra Pradesh, Karnataka plateau and highlands and interior parts of Tamil Nadu.

The text explains that planning and Sustainable Development in Indian Context 67 Target Area Planning The planning process has to take special care of those areas which have remained economically backward.

The text explains that as you know, the economic development of a region depends upon its resource base.

The text explains that but sometimes resource-rich region also remain backward.

The text explains that economic development requires technology, as well as, investment besides resources.

The text explains that some of the examples of programmes directed towards the development of target areas are Command Area Development Programme, Drought Prone Area Development Programme, Desert Development Programme, Hill Area Development Programme.

The text explains that in the 8th Five Year Plan special area programmes were designed to develop infrastructure in hill areas, north-eastern states, tribal areas and backward areas.

The text explains that these programmes aimed at harnessing the indigenous resources of the hill areas through development of horticulture,

The text explains that 68 India : People and Economy Case Study – Integrated Tribal Development Project in Bharmaur* Region Bharmaur tribal area comprises Bharmaur and Holi tehsils of Chamba district of Himachal Pradesh.

The text explains that bharmaur tribal region has harsh climate conditions, low resource base and fragile environment.

The text explains that these factors have influenced the society and Economy of the region.

The text explains that the economy is largely based on agriculture and allied activities such as sheep and goat rearing.

The text explains that the process of development of tribal area of Bharmaur started in 1970s when Gaddis were included among ‘scheduled tribes’.

The text explains that its mean monthly temperature in January remains 4°C and in July 26°C.

The text explains that the most significant contribution of tribal sub-plan in Bharmaur region is the development of infrastructure in terms of schools, healthcare facilities, potable water, roads, communications and electricity.

The text explains that 70 India : People and Economy beneficiaries of infrastructural development.

The text explains that the remote villages in Tundah and Kugti areas still do not have sufficient infrastructure.

The text explains that traditionally, the Gaddis had subsistence agricultural-cum-pastoral economy having emphasis on foodgrains and livestock production.

The text explains that the declining importance of pastoralism in the economy of the region can be gauged from the fact that at present only about one-tenth of the total households practise transhumance.

The text explains that sustainable Development Sustainable Development Sustainable Development Sustainable Development Sustainable Development The term development is generally used to describe the state of particular societies and the process of changes experienced by them.

The text explains that during a fairly large period of human history, the state of the societies has largely been determined by the interaction processes between human societies and their bio-physical environment.

The text explains that the processes of human- environment interaction depend upon the level of technology and institutions nurtured by a society.

The text explains that hence, development is a multi-dimensional concept and signifies the positive, irreversible transformation of the economy, society and environment.

The text explains that while dealing with the questions related to redistribution and equity, it was realised that the concept of development cannot be restricted to the economic sphere alone.

The text explains that it also includes the issues such as improving the well-being and living standard of people, availing of the health, education and equality of opportunity and ensuring political and civil rights.

The text explains that by 1980s, development emerged as a concept encapsulating wide-spread improvement in social as well as material well- being of all in a society.

The text explains that 72 India : People and Economy for conservation of resources to enable the future generations to use these resources.

The text explains that case Study Indira Gandhi Canal (Nahar) Command Area Indira Gandhi Canal, previously known as the Rajasthan Canal, is one of the largest canal systems in India.

The text explains that the total planned length of the system is 9,060 km catering to the irrigation needs of a total culturable command area of 19.63 lakh hectares.

The text explains that (i) What are the social benefits of ITDP in the Bharmaur tribal region?

The text explains that (ii) Define the concept of sustainable development.

The text explains that (iii) What are the positive impacts of irrigation on Indira Gandhi Canal Command Area?

The text explains that (i) Write short notes on drought-prone area programme.

The text explains that how does this programme help in the development of dryland agriculture in India?

Source: NCERT Class 12 India People and Economy, Chapter 6: Water Resources
